note: Tokyo's oldest temple; grounds fully paved, an elevator/ramp on the left reaches the main hall. Nakamise approach is flat but extremely crowded on weekends.
Wide paved paths and ramps throughout; gravel main path has a smooth paved strip for wheels/strollers. Serene, free entry, step-free to the shrine.
Large flat park, mostly accessible with rest spots; museums (Tokyo National, Western Art, Science) accessible & free for disabled + companion. Some temple buildings in-park have steps.
Japan's oldest zoo, next to Ueno Park; fully wheelchair accessible with enough accessible restrooms; giant pandas. Some hilly sections. Great with kids.

The world's busiest crossing; curb cuts and tactile paving everywhere and surrounding malls (Scramble Square, Hikarie) have elevators, but it's intensely crowded which is hard for seniors/manual chairs.
The inner garden within Meiji Shrine has gravel paths and can only be fully accessed via some stairs; only partly paved. The shrine itself is accessible but this garden is not.
Modern tower with full step-free access, wheelchair rentals, and priority elevator access to the observation decks. Easy back-to-back with Senso-ji.
Large, famous garden that is wheelchair accessible with wide flat paths; relaxed and great for families and cherry blossoms.
Trend districts; Omotesando and NW Shibuya streets are notably hilly, hard in a manual wheelchair, and Takeshita-dori is narrow and crowded. Department stores are accessible.
Two huge modern complexes that are wheelchair-friendly with accessible restaurants and metro stations; easy indoor wins.
Modern immersive digital-art venue, generally step-free; some dark/uneven immersive rooms. Confirm current venue accessibility when booking.
Bustling food-stall market; flat but very narrow and crowded aisles make wheels and seniors slow-going. Great food.
